lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of food and beverage processing machinery, including dairy, bakery, meat and poultry, and other commercial food-product processing equipment, plus manufacturer-attributable replacement, rebuild, parts, service, and monitoring revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Food and beverage packaging machinery, commercial and industrial refrigeration, commercial cooking and food-warming equipment, captive internal units, pure distributors, and service firms that do not manufacture the relevant machinery.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat outsourced revenue from external food and beverage processors through equipment programs, replacement units, aftermarket parts, rebuilds, maintenance, service agreements, leases, and manufacturer-linked software.

a.rationale: Current exposure lies in engineering documentation and reuse, quoting, purchasing, planning, scheduling, machining and welding assistance, assembly, machine-vision inspection, troubleshooting, and administrative work. Custom sanitary design, physical fabrication and integration, customer trials, accountable quality work, and field service keep most wages outside the presently substitutable set.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from a representative sample of food-machinery OEMs, separating already automated work from engineering, production, field-service, and office tasks, would materially move the range.
caveats: The BLS occupational table combines several machinery industries and occupation shares are not task shares.
JBT Marel is far larger and more digital than the target firms.
a.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS employment for combined NAICS 3331, 3332, 3334, and 3339, where production occupations were 48.45% and assemblers and fabricators were 19.37%, supplemented by NIST manufacturing use cases. Transformation: assigned plausible exposed fractions within production, engineering, service, sales, and office work and wage-weighted them conceptually, excluding already automated and accountable physical tasks. Error direction: the broad mix may overstate exposure if sanitary custom machinery requires more bespoke hands-on work, but may understate exposure to design reuse, CNC, robotic welding, and digital service.

rho.rationale: Implementation benefits from visible manufacturing use cases and customer demand for automation, monitoring, inspection, yield, sanitation, and labor efficiency. It remains incomplete because LMM firms face capital, data, skills, cybersecurity, validation, legacy integration, change-management, and low-volume customization constraints.
rho.change_evidence: MEP center implementation records or a PMMI/FPSA survey reporting completed deployments, spend, time to production, and realized labor hours by food-machinery OEM size would materially change the five-year implementation share.
caveats: NIST reports manufacturers generally rather than food-machinery OEMs.
Trade-association trends reflect customer-side processing automation as well as automation inside equipment manufacturers.
rho.bridge: Source population/quantity: NIST reports 46% of manufacturers using AI tools and more than 80% expecting increased use within two years; JBT Marel offers AI-enabled machine optimization and PMMI identifies rising AI, monitoring, inspection, and automation demand. Transformation: translated adoption direction into the implemented share of this industry's exposed task opportunity over five years, discounted for smaller-firm capital, integration, validation, and skills constraints. Error direction: stated plans and a leading OEM may overstate LMM implementation, while falling tool costs and customer automation requirements could make the central case conservative.

e.rationale: Many independent food-machinery manufacturers have repeat external customers and installed-base revenue from parts, rebuilds, service, upgrades, leases, or replacement machines. Ineligible firms include one-off fabricators without repeat revenue, captive operations, pure distributors or integrators, service-only firms, and businesses actually classified in packaging, refrigeration, or commercial cooking equipment.
e.change_evidence: A Census Business Register or proprietary company-list review linking establishment products, ownership, recurring-revenue mix, and normalized EBITDA would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: No source measures the share of LMM firms simultaneously meeting the exact NAICS, independence, external-customer, and repeat-revenue tests.
A leading OEM's 50% recurring mix should not be assumed for smaller manufacturers.

s5.rationale: Private employer businesses face material succession pressure, but a qualifying transfer requires a completed control change in a viable eligible firm. Planned sales can be delayed or become closures, minority financings, nonqualifying family transitions, or transactions outside the target band.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, and industry-broker closed transactions matched to 333241, plus owner-age and succession-plan interviews among PMMI/FPSA member OEMs, would materially refine the probability.
caveats: Gallup covers employer-business owners across industries rather than this NAICS or company-size band.
Intentions are not completed qualifying transfers.
s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: Gallup's fall 2024 survey found that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Transformation: used that intention rate as the central anchor and widened for completion risk and possible aging-owner concentration in specialized private machinery firms. Error direction: plans likely overstate completed qualifying transfers, while unplanned health, partnership, or strategic sales create some offsetting undercount.

q.rationale: Fixed-price equipment and service work can retain implemented savings until rebid, and installed-base compatibility, food-safety knowledge, uptime, parts availability, proprietary controls, and service responsiveness support value capture. Large processor sourcing programs, competitive local suppliers, negotiated renewals, customer demands for shared savings, and cost-focused procurement reduce retention over five years.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level data from LMM OEMs showing fixed-price versus cost-plus terms, renewal cadence, realized pricing, bid win rates, customer concentration, gross margin by new equipment and aftermarket, and explicit productivity-sharing clauses would materially move the range.
caveats: The range concerns discounted retained gross benefit rather than accounting margin.
JBT Marel's scale, intellectual property, installed base, and service network likely provide more pricing protection than many LMM firms.

d5.rationale: Food consumption is defensive, while persistent processor labor shortages, sanitation, food safety, yield, sustainability, and inspection needs support equipment and aftermarket demand. Customer capital cycles, high rates, processor consolidation, crop or livestock shocks, and the possibility that nominal market growth mostly reflects price and mix constrain real quantity growth.
d5.change_evidence: Annual PMMI/FPSA shipment units and constant-quality price indexes by equipment category through 2031, paired with U.S. food-processor capital-expenditure plans and installed-base replacement ages, would materially refine year-five real demand.
caveats: The trade-association forecast reaches 2027 rather than the full five-year horizon and reports shipment value, not constant-price constant-quality quantity.
JBT Marel is global, diversified, acquisition-affected, and much larger than the U.S. LMM population.
d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: PMMI/FPSA reports the U.S. food and beverage processing machinery market at $6.2 billion of shipments in 2025, up 3.2%, and forecasts $6.7 billion in 2027; JBT Marel reports resilient aftermarket demand and diversified equipment demand. Transformation: converted the near-term nominal shipment direction into a five-year real-quantity interval by removing an implicit price and mix component, extending modestly, and widening for capital cycles and replacement timing. Error direction: nominal shipment value likely overstates real quantity growth, while accelerating labor-saving automation, safety investment, or replacement could make the extension too low.

o.rationale: Food processors still require physical machinery that an accountable manufacturer designs, fabricates, integrates, validates, warrants, and supports. Software and AI enhance equipment and service rather than eliminate the OEM function; only a small share is vulnerable to customer self-design, imports, standardized component substitution, or migration to suppliers outside the exact lens.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews and lost-order analyses quantifying in-house builds, direct imports, software-only substitutions, third-party service displacement, and OEM-free equipment architectures would materially move the surviving operator-required share.
caveats: This measures persistence of the accountable operator role, not survival of any incumbent.
Integrated software and third-party equipment support can shift value between machinery OEMs, software vendors, integrators, and processors without eliminating physical equipment demand.

narrative.executive_view: Food-product machinery has a defensible physical role and recurring installed-base economics, with meaningful but bounded automation inside OEM workflows. Demand is supported by defensive food production and processor automation needs, though capital spending and subsegment shocks remain material.
narrative.ai_impact: AI, machine vision, digital twins, design reuse, predictive maintenance, scheduling, quoting, documentation, and robotics can reduce engineering, production, inspection, and support labor, but sanitary customization, trials, field integration, and quality accountability constrain substitution.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed-price work and installed-base parts, rebuild, software, and service relationships support retention; processor sourcing initiatives, rebids, local competition, and negotiated renewals gradually share savings with customers.
narrative.firm_availability: The exact industry includes many specialized OEMs with repeat external customers, but eligibility falls when firms are one-off fabricators, captive, distribution-led, or actually belong to packaging, refrigeration, or cooking-equipment codes. Broad owner evidence indicates a material but uncertain succession pool.
narrative.demand_durability: Trade-association evidence points to continued U.S. shipment growth, reinforced by food safety, sanitation, labor scarcity, yield, and sustainability needs. Real quantity can nevertheless pause during processor capital restraint or agricultural and protein-market shocks.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are processor consolidation and buyer power, fixed-price overruns, tariffs and steel costs, food recalls and animal disease, customer capital deferrals, technology obsolescence, service competition, imports, and extrapolation from large global companies.
narrative.principal_driver: A large installed base creates repeat parts, rebuild, maintenance, software, and replacement demand around safety-critical physical equipment.
narrative.principal_weakness: Specialized customer capital projects are lumpy, and large processors can exert strong sourcing and repricing pressure.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual
sources.supports: Exact 333241 scope and exclusions for packaging machinery, refrigeration equipment, and commercial cooking equipment.

sources.supports: Broad machinery-manufacturing occupational mix used as the task-exposure proxy.

sources.supports: Current manufacturing AI adoption, demonstrated use cases, investment direction, and implementation barriers.

sources.supports: Five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners.

sources.supports: Installed-base and recurring-revenue model, service and digital offerings, fixed-price contract structure, sourcing pressure, backlog, demand conditions, and industry risks.

sources.title: PMMI and FPSA Release Inaugural 2026 Processing State of the Industry Report and Infographic
sources.supports: U.S. food and beverage processing machinery shipment value, near-term forecast, end-market mix, and automation, safety, AI, and sustainability trends.
